words: So that wasn't in the same structure that the temple had?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, no. No. This other one was very rude. In fact, the first time that I read this paper, there were two or three women there who knew of this. And they thought that it was, one of them said she thought it had a tin roof, and the other one thought that it just had canvas over it. But I am sure that this wasn't true, because they, as I say, they had zero weather during this time. It was six below zero one night. And I, and beside, you know, our climate is not such that they could have just, it couldn't have been a tent. And beside, they tell in here about bringing in the lumber from Felton's Mill out there by wherever it is. So, and I think that it was roofed over, and the night that these college students came, they threw rocks up on the roof. And as they rolled down, it created such a din inside that you couldn't hear anything. Now, this could have been tin, you know, put on top of that, that could have been. And, but it was in the height of this uproar about the university students. And of course, I think that, you know, his attack was just in general about the sin and so on. But he zeroed in on the editor of the Argonaut and the fellow who wrote this editorial, Stalker, and the band that played Darden Noah.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Which is the Mann Brothers?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes. He just had to catch their act here in Moscow. I don't know whether he'd ever heard them or not. He just picked up on us. I don't know. Well, you know. And I don't know whether he'd ever gone to any of their dances or not, I would question that.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: But, and this man was from Colfax, wasn't he? The leader of the orchestra?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, some way or other, see, Volgen had come here, I believe, from Colfax. And, and some way or other, and of course he'd gotten into it over in Colfax with the American Legion. And so, it some way sticks in my memory that this orchestra leader was from Colfax. But I could have been wrong about that. You said that before they put the tabernacle there, there'd been victory garden.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Do you remember much about that at all?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, except that it was planted to vegetables.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: All the people?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I guess, well, I don't really understand. I know a little bit about victory gardens, but... Well, I think then, as in World War II, that it was considered to be, you know, an ecological, a patriotic thing to put gardens into every vacant lot. Oh, I see. And this was right downtown. I don't know who did it, whether it was, whether post office employees volunteered or whether, you know, but it was a patriotic gesture, and it had been a victory garden. And, and they just finished it. It had been plowed up, and it...

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: The handling of saving food in World War I was much, much different from the way it was in World War II. There was no such thing as ratchet stands. As a matter of fact, about all there was really was publicity. There wasn't any, any such control of prices as there was during World War II, with the result that the scarce commodities went up and priced very, very rapidly.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And the greatest pressure was upon people to save wheat and meat. There were eggless days. There were milkless days. There were butterless days. There were meatless days.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: During World War I.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: World War I, yes.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: So that practically, practically it was what we would call job owning today, that together with this relentless rise in price.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And I would say this, that, that, of course the real shortages encompassed the major staples. But I can't say that there was anything, any very severe shortages outside of that. Wheat was, wheat and bread was a much more substantial part of the daily diet than we know it today.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: There were so, there were so many things that we take as a matter, as a matter of course today that simply, simply did not exist. One example, for example, is Chile. Chile came in around 1919 and it was a craze.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: The small towns that sprout a chili parlor and the, Bob's Chili Parlor in Spokane made a fortune. And then eventually people were just charmed to find that they could actually buy chili parlor in stores. Prior to that time the only way you could get chili was to go to one of these chili parlors. And they, with the chili parlors, why they, they brought the mollies also.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But anything in the way of, of ethnic cuisine, cookery, was almost wholly non -existent then. I don't know how old I was before I had my first Chinese meal,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: for example.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And things like, things like pizza, bar, far in the future.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, there's interest in like, chili and things like that.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Like you said, they had a parlor in Spokane.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did they have anything like that here in Moscow?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes, there was a Bob's Chili Parlor in here.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it like a, a franchise?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, not that I know of. I, I don't know. You know, they must have bought from wholesalers, but it was, um, um, where, um, Anderson and Felton and whatever his name is that, that law office is now, right there on the alley. Jerry's was across the alley, and it was a confectionery and cigar store. And yes, it was a chili parlor, and there was a, um, stairway going up to a balcony. And, uh, I can't remember who the Bob was. I mean, that was his real name. Bob Woods was. Bob Woods, yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Well, he used to, he used to run the Blue Bucket for years, didn't he?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Now, the Blue Bucket was private, was privately owned, and it was the nucleus of what is now the Student Union Building.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Wasn't that, uh, didn't Dean French that?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She built it, I believe.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, it seems, I don't know, kind of odd, in a place like Moscow where the soil's really nice. It seems like everybody would have had gardens at that time.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why would they have a victory garden?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, I think maybe that was just, um, again, you know, just publicity, just a reminder that you couldn't go downtown, you see, without seeing a big victory garden.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: It was the idea of, here we have one right downtown, what are you doing, you know, with your backyard? It was, was that the purpose behind it, to promote patriotic ideas?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I think so, yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Uh -huh, uh -huh, just a reminder, and, um, probably that was federally owned, wasn't it?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I think maybe that whole block was owned.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: That's a lot of stuff. I don't know.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was the, was the, what was done with the goods that came out of the garden?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I don't know. I, I have no idea.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I don't know. Twenty -eight when I came here, the Christian Church was there. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And then how, how long did the Christian Church stand?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, till about ten years ago, I guess.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: They moved, they consolidated with another church, and it stood for a while, and there was as much discussion as there is now about, you know, what we do with the, with the Christian Church.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And, uh, and eventually, uh, they, I guess the city bought it, didn't they? Who? The city, didn't they city by that and enter it down for the parking lot?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But it was a brick building, and not as old as a lot of the other churches.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It seems like, for as long as Moscow's been here, there's been quite a few buildings up and down, and so many changes in the house.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Sometimes I'm not quite sure where everything was, people would say, oh, this was here. There was some New York writer, a few years back, wrote, they're tearing down buildings that I saw built. And I never thought that that time would come to me this year. Yeah. I know.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I see it too. I haven't lived in Moscow all that long, maybe about five years, and I always get surprised when a building comes down and it's not there anymore. I used to see it.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But, it's changing a lot, what with shopping centers going on.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Sometimes I think it doesn't seem like Idaho people sometimes really have a hold on Moscow like they used to have.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It seems like there's a lot of outside interest maybe coming in.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I don't know, maybe that's the way it's always been. I don't know.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Well, where's the local?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: There's some local changes. The Main Street businessmen don't have the clout that they used to be. There were two or three of them that were archdukes, and the rest were dukes, or a good many of the rest. And then perhaps a few more of lower gentry, but they put their heads together and decided what to do, what to advocate.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But, the coming of the first of the supermarkets outside control, now the shopping malls, the Main Street businessman is pressed just to hold his own. And so far as political influence is concerned, it is a thing of the past. He is relegated to the position of ordinary citizen, and it wasn't that way 50 years ago. It just wasn't that way. They ran the show.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: When the chips were down, they had a substantial voice in university policy if it was anything that concerned them. And in return, they took care of the political needs of the university. The university wasn't doing its own politicking in those days. It was done by the Main Street businessman in Moscow.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I think that is an alliance which has just slowly faded away. It faded away, of course, because the Main Street businessman no longer has any such clout.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And they no longer enjoy the unique position with reference to the university that they once did. Everything that the university buys now, practically without exception, is done on open bits. It wasn't that way once. And the rest of the state didn't get much of a chance about this. It was placed with some local businessman. There was no student bookstore.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Where did students buy their books?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: A Hodgins drugstore.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Hodgins was responsible for getting in all this kind of things.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Wouldn't any other store sell the books? Would it just be like that one?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It was just Hodgins.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: After a while, there was a Sherfys.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But they didn't handle the university books. You see, all the textbooks, the system isn't going to work unless the teacher certifies what the book is and sends it to the book dealer, and then he orders it from the publisher. Now, if you've got, let us suppose, 24 on a class, that is about the number of books that a dealer would want to order. Now, you can't very well have a competitive set -up with two or more dealers. You're going to have a lot of leftover merchandise. So that all the textbooks, and so on, that was handled through Hodgins.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Wiley Lawder had all the cement business sold out. Nobody in Latah County could buy as much as a pound of cement on a book.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: From WH Lawder. I'm going to say with WA, W .A. Lawder.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I just feel like it's a real change to see those shopping malls going up, as opposed to the independent business people here in Moscow. I think the funny part of it was, too, that Mr. Kenner, who was the kingpin of the Kings, really was the one who broke it wide open when he, I think, when he built the Safeway shopping center. He bought up all that property behind his theaters.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And before that, Safeway was just a hole in the wall like any other grocery. It was no supermarket.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: But it was a Safeway?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: There was one here. It was on Main Street.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: An occupied part of what's now the Penny Place.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But as I say, it was no supermarket. But he built this building and leased it to them over the long -term lease. I mean, eight on a long -term lease. But they successfully fended off Sears and Montgomery Ward. In fact, all of the downtown business property was so closely held. See, they couldn't find a place to be. And this was before the day of the shopping mall, when you took your car out and parked there. And this was still the time of the local deliveries, when you could call up and have the groceries sent out to your house.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But I think that Safeway broke that pattern. You see, come and get it yourself and pay cash. And there were no more charge accounts and there were no more deliveries. I mean, there weren't at Safeways.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And then once they got this bigger store with the place to park, why?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Even the Faithful deserted the others then. The people that would give you credit. I would think that people wouldn't want that kind of system.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Because they knew that if they couldn't pay their bills one month, they could pay them an exit.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: You said they'd have to pay it right then. As far as I know, there's only two left. The Modern Way, which will take some. And a 30 -day accounts and a little IGA store. And that's it.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Why do you think people found that new system more preferable than a store that would give them credit?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I mean, there was that few -sense difference in the price. And as I say, even some of the people who were in the closely held community, you know, eventually broke down and went to these other places. And, of course, you didn't have the choice in the smaller stores with the charge accounts. They didn't carry as many lines. I mean, they carried one line of groceries, like round -up groceries, and if you didn't like that kind of canned tomatoes, that was tough. So it was more like people would rather have that choice. And be able to choose among several brands for several prices.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: When did that so -called new building go up?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I don't know.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What new building? What would that say?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Safeways, the present one.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, now there have been two buildings on that location. Yes, I know. The first one was built some time in the latter 30s. I'm going to say 1937 or 38. And it didn't occupy all what ground that it does now.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It came up to the corner of Fifth and Washington. And the building itself was, the front was right directly on Fifth. You walked from the street right into it. The parking lot then was alongside, and it was much smaller. And in the rear of the Safeway premises, there was something originally built as a private house that Dr. Zimmett was using as his cat and dog hospital, as well as his home. And then on the other side of that, on the corner, a small brick garage. And that was where old, who's Lowell, the tractor, and Harold Lowell. I would always have my tractor down there to be repaired. I'd forgotten. Harold Lowell was in the State Senate for a number of years, too. They were on the corner. But the Safeways obviously was cramped. It wasn't enough space for the store, it wasn't enough space for parking. So that Ken Rody was eventually able to pick up these other two pieces of property.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And the present building then, I'm sure that it was built only after the war, but not too long afterward. I'm going to say sometime prior to 1950, anyhow.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And if I remember correctly,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: they built the new building without tearing down any of the old. That is, the new building is sighted in the rear where the old building was.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: The old building was where parking lot was, fronting directly on Fifth Street. So that it must have been quite shallow. And the new building, of course, as I say, was further back, and it took the rest of the block all the way back.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was that the first franchise here in Moscow? A store that would, you know, have Safeways?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, it's been a penny store here ever since I can remember.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: That was here before Safeways had been here?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Mm -hmm. I would say that it was. As a matter of fact, prior to Safeway, there was a Piggly Wiggly. Piggly Wiggly became Safeway. When I was in college, there was a Piggly Wiggly store on the ground floor, what's now the two apartments, where the ground furniture is. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: That's the ground furniture.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: The problem never went in it.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you come here to go to college? To go to law school?

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Were you originally from Idaho?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, I was born in the state of Washington.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I went to high school and had two years of college in Spokane.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But my mother had died, and at the time I came down here to law school, I, uh, the family broke up housekeeping. My father moved down to southern Idaho to involves, which was my legal residence in the time that I was in school. And, uh, you remember down there.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And that's what brought you up to the University of Idaho Law School, was that you had been living in Idaho?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Prior to Piggly Wiggly?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: When you were in law school, were you, um, which was the building that you were taking classes in?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It was in the ed building.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: The one that they were in just before building the new law school?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, yes, but they, uh, the way they, the way they, the old law school.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: When I was in, when I was in law school, we were up on the second floor of the ed building, right at the head of the stairs. The library consisted of two rooms there in the ed building. The, uh, Dean, uh, the Dean had an office of his own. All the professors were crammed into one room. And they had two classrooms then. I'm sure there were two. And that was all the space they had. The, uh, the faculty consisted of four persons, including the Dean. But, uh, I would say that even, uh,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: the traditions that they had in those days, uh,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: involved a high standard scholarship. In the early 1920s, law school was a pipe. That's where all the athletes were enrolled.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Really?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes, that was in the early 1920s. And sometime in the mid -1920s, that was changed. It may be that Dean Davis, who was the Dean the first year that I came here, was brought in to affect that change. And, uh, of the man that he had there, there was Jacob. Davis came from Harvard. Jacob came from Harvard. Moreau was from Wisconsin. I've forgotten who the fourth member of it was, but it was, it was heavily flavored with Harvard tradition. And, uh, the standard of scholarship that they, uh, that some of those men insisted upon was white rigorous.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did Howard come here as a Dean, or did he come as a teacher first?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I think he came as a professor. Oh, yes. The fourth member of that first year was Masterson, and he was Harvard. But, uh, I think it was Jacob who told us this story. I think this might have been a very first class session. That something is, something that he said was told to all Harvard freshmen law students. The first day. Take a good look at the man on your right, and at the man on your left. Because at the end of the first semester, one of you probably won't be here.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But, uh, there were, there were two moves. Sometime, I can't pinpoint this date with precision, but it was sometime in the 1930s. The law school was moved up to the third or top floor on the south wing of the Ade Building. The library was still in the, was still in the, uh, it was clear in the, in the, uh, in the, uh, east, or westernmost portions. And I remember the afternoon sun would come in there, and there weren't shades so much that some of the buckram book, uh, bindings of the law books were just literally bleached, and I dare say they still are. Those are the, those are the books that are left. They were in that location. Well, this time the library, I think, was still in one room, but it was a somewhat bigger room. And, uh, I think they had, uh,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: two office rooms for professors, rather than one. They also had, um, they also had two classrooms, as before. I taught for one semester before going into the service. I taught up there. That was in 1943, the spring of 1943. Well, then during the, during the war, when I was gone, why things were just, uh, it was just strictly a maintenance or holding operation. During, I, I don't know whether this was during both years of the war, or that is from 1943, 44, and 44, and 45, or just one. There were four students.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Needless to say, they said as little to that as possible to the legislature. And they were trying to keep their, trying to keep the law school together as a going, as a going proposition. Well, I got up in September of 1945, and I came back to teach, and still at the, still up on the, still up on the third floor there. And, uh, it was crowded, and it was unsatisfactory. The, uh, University Library was on the first floor of the ed building, on the ground floor, in the south wing.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And somebody decided that if you would, when, when the new University Library building was built, that if the law school would take over that, they could use these library stacks that were there. So, that space was remodeled, and this time there was, there was much, the biggest, uh, growth in the amount of space they had in the law school. They had all these stacks from the, from the old general library. And, uh, they had, they had three classrooms, including one that was a courtroom. They had a whole lot more office space, they had one for each professor, and they had some, some, uh, uh, better accommodations for the, uh, for the secretarial help, and for the librarian. But, uh, in the meantime, apparently the enrollment had been growing like Jackrath's when I was, uh, when I was in college in the 19, in 28 to 31 law school. The law school enrollment was about 30, and we were graduating about 10 a year.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: By 1946 or 47, the enrollment was up somewhere around, like, somewhere around, uh, 75 or thereabouts. Uh, today I understand it's around 300, but then it passed 250. But, the, this, uh, the space on the, the space in the ad building, why that was, uh, that was something magnificent compared to anything that we'd had before. But, what happened is that the enrollment just outgrew it, and, uh, made the new building just about a, uh, just about a necessity.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What was the year that the law school was started here?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It's a fairly early school. I think that it, I think that it goes back, um, I'm gonna say sometime around 1905, 1908, thereabouts.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And do you know where that, that faculty was from originally? Do I, pardon me? Do you know where that faculty was from originally? Were they also from Mackie's?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I don't know. They, uh...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So I was thinking, you know, where else would the lawyers be getting educated in this area? Do you know if there was, like, a law school at any of the other university schools?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I'm sure that's, I'm sure that's why the, uh, the law school has always had its, uh, had the support that it has had. Was the feeling that it would be too bad to have the... not to, not to have, uh, a, uh, local school. I know that after all does have some, some, uh, some things in the law that are, uh, uh, peculiar to itself. Well, not exactly, but at any rate, uh, uh, differ from the general run. We've got some differences in our community property theory from, from any that you will find anywhere else.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But, uh... I'm sure that, I'm sure the feeling was that if we had to rely upon, or rely upon both for, uh, our judges, upon men that were trained exclusively outside the state, that they might not have either the interest in or the knowledge of local problems.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I'm sure that entered into the thinking somewhere along the line.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What were some of the things that were special interest or that were special problems?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: That, uh, the eye of the law of people would have to take.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, irrigation and water rights, for example, is one.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Uh... Well, when I was in, when I was at law school, we didn't have any course in irrigation, irrigation and water rights, and we didn't have any course in mining law, but they usually would have somebody come in and give us a course in my lectures. Somebody who was reasonable, well -informed in that, uh, field. And we got something out of it. Well, the day, of course, that has long since been, uh, long since been changed. And, uh, in putting together curriculums and the, and writing the books, why they've telescoped, uh, mining rights and water rights. And the law students have their own, have their own name for the course. That course they call rocks and water.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But, uh, it's, it's something that's a, it's something that's a basic, uh, basic importance to the state and, uh, something that you're, uh, our Western theory of, of water rights is completely different from the common law theory. The result is that somebody, somebody trained in the East or even in the Middle West wouldn't, wouldn't have a, uh, an adequate understanding of the doctrine of appropriation, which obtains throughout the Western states. And the, uh, in the East, the common law doctrine of riparian rights is something that, uh, was simply borrowed from the common law of England. That if a stream is flowing through somebody's property, he has a right to, to, um, make use of the, uh, of the, uh, stream. But that essentially he has to return water to the stream substantially on the main study. He can't, uh, he can't, uh, drain it and deprive the, uh, owner below of, uh, his similar riparian rights.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Now, in countries where there's, areas where there's plenty of water, that system works fine. But in areas where, in, in arid areas wherein, uh, you can make land productive only through irrigation, that becomes completely impossible. So there was adopted the, uh, the, um, developed the doctrine of appropriation. That the water right belongs to the man who first appropriates it and puts it to a useful purpose.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And when he appropriates it, develops it, puts it to a useful purpose, the people below can't do anything about it, and the people above have got to leave his water alone.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So it's whoever puts the stream in the water?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: His first, it's hers.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was that a problem that you faced a great deal here in Idaho?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes, the, uh, the, uh, the scheme of, uh, you've got a very complex, uh, your water rights in southern Idaho depend upon, depend upon, uh, decisions in a number of, of individual cases, adjudicating what the, uh, what the, uh, rights are, and it's effect upon the economy, the, the area is, of course, enormous. Now there's, there's an area south of Twin Falls called the Salmon Tract. Pretty fair land, not the best in the tract. But the point is that they've got a limited water, a limited water right only when there's water left over. So as to speak, that some of the prior appropriators don't use, then the result is that that land is under plow only about one year and three. And the other years it grows up to weeds. You have land north of the, uh, Snake River. It's your own country, your own goody. Some years, that is not farmed either. Because there doesn't happen to be any water that year. The Twin Falls Tract itself has got perhaps as, as good and substantial a water right. Not only in the, uh, it's legal security, but in a mall. There's any place in the state. And, uh, it's lush and it produces like a garden of Eden.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Can I, uh, I'd like to ask you some things about when you were a student here.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: It looks like, um, where, where were you living when you first came up here to be a student?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I was at Lindley Hall during the time I was in college. That's now a university parking lot. It's been torn down a couple of years ago.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, when you were here in 28, that's pretty near out of the depression.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Does that have any influence on while you were going to school? Did you feel that as far as, like, having a way to go to school, or did you have to have jobs of some kind?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I worked, I worked, uh, I worked part -time downtown all the time when I was in law school. So I, uh, I saw a good deal more of, uh, life downtown than most university students did. And, uh,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: there's no question, there's no question about the effect of the depression. I would say that in one way we were isolated from it. It was before the, um, there weren't many radios. And, uh, news broadcasting, as we know it today, uh, didn't exist then, even on radio. And we didn't take newspapers. We didn't see newspapers. Literally, we had very little idea what was going on in the outside world. I know that my father was very hard -pressed during those, uh, during those years. I had very little money to get along on. That's, uh, I had to earn a major part of my expenses. And, uh, I was perhaps lucky to have any help at all, and really lucky to be able to make a two -law school, uh, in a rush.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How much was tuition in those days to go?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, it was very small compared with what it is today. I am, uh, I doubt if, uh, I doubt a general fee is ran over, uh, $50 a semester. I'm sure they didn't.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And then there would be room rent.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Pardon me, yes?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: There would be room rent, and you'd have to buy books. Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So you would have to, like you said, you'd have to work pro -time?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And you would have to pay for the full time that you were going to school?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yeah. The, uh,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: the room rent would be paid to the University of the semester at a time in advance. And, uh, the board bills, you could pay as it can do. Of course, some guys got behind on that.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you attend the University, too, this year?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you go all four years as well?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: What did you study?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I was an English major, and my player went into teaching.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you teach in this area?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I went...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you two meet when you were going to school?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: We just never happened to.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: It was half of after you were out of school?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes, it was after I had taught in South Idaho and I came home. My father knew him, but I didn't. No, I just never happened to meet. Well, for one thing, he was in the law school, so he wasn't, and had not been an undergraduate here, so he didn't really have the acquaintance, you know, that you would have if you had started in as a freshman and gone all over.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: That's the way through. The law school was a little world all to itself.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It's kind of still that way.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: There wasn't too much rubbing your shoulders with the rest of the student population.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But the ones who go through undergraduate here, you know, have an acquaintance that you wouldn't have if you just came into a professional school like that.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you have to work to go to school, too? Did you have to have a job of any kind so that you could go to school?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was he able to go?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I lived at home, and very few at that time, practically none of boys or girls who lived in town ever lived on campus. Well, for one thing, our folks couldn't afford it. And another thing, they didn't encourage that because those houses and dormitories were built for out -of -town students, not ones who lived here in town. And so it was almost unheard of for anybody. Oh, for instance, if your folks went away for a semester or a year or something like that, why you maybe could get in.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But housing was tight enough that local students weren't... I was a city attorney. Margaret's father was mayor and appointed me city attorney. I knew him long for some years before I knew her. But anyhow, there was always some argument about student housing in town.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And Carl Smith, who was the fire chief, he felt that a lot of these places where there's student housing upstairs should have an outside stairway to provide an exit in the event of fire. He, however, came into conflict with the indomitable Dean French, Dean of Women, who felt that the outside stairway represented a threat to the morals of the students. I won't say that there ever was any settlement of the outside stairway problem, but you'll see around town some houses that have got such stairways and others that don't.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She must have been quite a woman.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I've heard a few people talk about Dean French and said that when they were going to school here, she was something to be feared, but as soon as they graduated, they had a great deal of respect for her.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I think that she took it that it was her job, at least as far as the girls are concerned, that she was in local parenthesis. And if parenthesis felt that way too, that she was. And I think that she, I am sure, would feel that if she hadn't done everything that she could in the way of enforcing rule, that parents would have held her responsible. And it was small enough enrollment, and enough of them who came, I think, were early supporters of the university, and maybe not graduates themselves. But I think that she did what the Morries of the time demanded, and that they had housed some mothers at the sorority houses, well, they had housemothers at the dormitories too. And it was not just the status thing. I mean, those housemothers were there to enforce the rules. The boys, of course, had a lot more freedom than housemothers that they had usually either didn't live there, just came in for stated occasions, or else they lived in a little apartment where they saw nothing or heard nothing or were told nothing. But the girls were closely supervised. And I think there were very few girls on campus who ever had cars.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: You know, that was almost done. There were not many male students who had cars either. There were some. They were, they were, they were, came from relatively affluent families. But the student who had a car was, was a rare. There weren't very many of them.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, if you were from the town, then you probably wouldn't be in the sororities. Very many women from here would. Some of them were because they had, either had kind of a great point that the sorority would like to add to theirs, or their parents had big homes and could help entertain him all, or who had silver services that they could borrow and the furniture and that sort of thing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And then there were, what did they call them?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: The women who, patronesses, I believe, that lived in town, married women who, why or why not, had families and they helped with the entertaining, I'm sure. And there were girls who came from that kind of families who had, you know, whose parents could donate something of that sort.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And then I think maybe the children of both boys and girls of faculty members tended to join because their parents had belonged at their schools, or maybe they were going on to school from here and they wanted to have that connection.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it much of the status to belong to the sororities and fraternities at that time?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, yes, I think it was, because their housing was much more elegant than the dormitories were, or than most of our homes were.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And they, they were all around.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, the fraternities and sororities, too, had a good deal more organized social activity than you would find. The town men's and women's organizations and the dormitories did have social activities and had social organizations, but there wasn't as much up there.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: So it would be desirable then to...

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yes, there was some point to it. There was some point to it.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I think they had guests. There were just dinner exchanges every week, you know, with the others and with the ones who lived on campus and who belonged to fraternities and sororities, and they had lots of dances and informal and formal and whatnot. And they could have them small enough then they could have them right in their houses, you know. And so, yes, they had a social life that was, you know, that was a thing apart from the ones who... And even, I don't think that even the dormitories had dances so often, but it was sort of a period of, you know, where the dances were the, and the dinners were the main social functions.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it something that most people found desirable to be in a fraternity or sorority?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, I think so. What was the status thing, I'm sure.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Did you try to pledge a sorority?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I suppose that there were ways, you know, influences that you could have used, but if there were, I didn't know what they were, and my parents didn't come from that kind of background. They were small town people and they went off to college, graduates, and as I say, if there were ways to make your wage felt and I suppose if I had had lots of money and lots of clothes and a car and that sort of thing, then I wasn't driving a car, I think, so I was a junior in a year and a boss.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And it was, it's funny, I mean, there are other status, you know, in the place that they might be, if I seems to change.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: You might be interested in one, in one true story about the sorority rushing.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: The year this must have been, what was the name of this gal from the Liston that became the famous singer?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Bollinger.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Yeah, and Bollinger.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: This must have been around, was it in the 30s?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, it was later than that.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Pardon me?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It was later than that. Yes, I'm going to put it now in the 40s, sometime around 1947, 1948, thereabouts.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: The system, the rushing system of the employed, it isn't something at the end of it, they called it the lawyer system and I was the lawyer that was, that drew this happy assignment. It was,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: essentially, every girl that was interested in pledging a sorority would fill out a little slip, indicating the sorority of her, sorority's upper choice in order of preference, one, two, three. And the sorority organization, all this was hush -hush, that is, it was very secret, nobody could see any other records of anybody else's. And the rushing organization in each sorority would send me a list of the girls that they wanted, also in order of their preference. Now, my job in the first place was to match them up. Actually, it turned out to be simpler than I had expected because it was all cut and dry, that is, this had all obviously been arranged before it hit me. So that in practically every instance it was a first choice match against a first choice.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And then the girls who were invited to pledge, I had to send out by messenger, they had to reach their residence houses at a certain hour later in that day. It was a very busy day, I can assure you, by messenger.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, when I was in the midst of this, I got an anguished phone call from Ann Bolinger that she had indicated her preference as Kappa Kappa Gamma, but that she wasn't familiar with the names of the sororities. What she had really intended to put down was Delta Gamma.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And would I do that?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, I said, I'm going to have to consult with the dean of women first.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And as I say, Ann assured me on the telephone, she just didn't realize which house was which. So I took it up with the dean of women, and I'd forgotten who she was then. And she said, the campus had gotten everybody that they asked for, but the Delta Gamma's haven't. So why not let the Delta Gamma's have her, if it's agreeable at all.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: So, I sent out the invites that way, but it seems as if, Ann, somewhere along the line, not knowing exactly what her status was because I hadn't promised her anything. I was sympathetic, it was a matter of receiving attention, but I didn't give her any decision, but that matter, no decision had been made. And she got Annicky thinking she was going nowhere. She was at one of the halls at the time. And she left the hall, I think, to go down to the Student Union Building for her evening meal.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And while she was there, somebody from the Kappa House saw her, noted that she was distraught and started asking questions. And of course she broke down.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, this ended with a grand confrontation.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: It was in my office that Dean of Women was there. The rushing chairman of both the Kappa House and the Delta Gamma's were there, and Ann was there. And I brought out the slip that she had said, and as I say, I had listed the Kappa House first.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And she cried a little bit. Well, now after this, she wasn't sure that the Kappa's would want her.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: They assured her that they did. And the Dean of Women was satisfied that it had not been any clerical mistake on her part. It was simply that she got bucked. She was somewhere along the line and changed her mind. And as I say, if it had been a clerical error, I'm sure that everybody would have seen fit to correct it. But changing her mind, now that was not supposed to be in accordance with the rules of this particular game. And the Delta Gamma's very courteously accepted the situation. That was the end of it, except there were a few odd remarks later in the Argonaut. I think it was at the time of the annual song and stunt fest that Ann had turned out and sang some sort of a song. My heart belongs to Delta Gamma. The Argonaut publishing the account of this, but in parenthesis.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Or is it Delta Gamma?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But you know, of course, what happened to Ann since.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Nothing at all. Great enough.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She became an opera singer.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: And where was she doing most of her singing?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, she sang a lot in Europe, but she did sing with the Met. She made her debut with the Met.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She married a musician in Switzerland. No, honey, he wasn't.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: He was an employee of Dow Chemical or something. He was a big chemical company. He was Norwegian or Danish, but he was stationed there. And she was doing a lot of concertizing around there. And she met him and they lived at Zurich. That's where she was living. I think she was one of the stars at the Zurich Opera.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But she sang around in Europe generally.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She came down with cancer. She gave several concerts at Lewis in her hometown. And this must have been about, this is about ten years ago.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She came back to Lewiston to give her last concert. And she knew it was her last and everybody in the audience knew it.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: She had two small children. I think they were four and six or six and eight or something like that. And she had had them over here. Her mother lived in Lewiston. But it was a sad situation because Mrs. Boelinger had these two very talented children. And Bob Boelinger sang with the Fred Waring organization. And not too long before that. And he also belonged to the Air National Guard and he was killed in flame crash. So she had these two children and Mrs. Boelinger outlived them by years. Both of them. I don't think Anne was any more than 40 when she died if that. I think she was still in her 30s. But she went back and she died in Zurich. She figured it would be there. And I guess that her husband still lives there. He seems to have been a...

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I'm probably about the only person who knows all about this little irregularity about getting her into a surreptit.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So did she finally get into Delta camp?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Camp of House. I'm just kidding.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Certain houses, you know, almost pitch for certain things. And I think that the cap was particularly...

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: How long has gone for the singers?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: They just seem to recruit for that. And some of them on the other hand want raids. And some of them want, you know, the welcome ones that were in place. That sort of thing. And for particular talents. But I have not a number of them who... You know, it gets that kind of a reputation idea.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Would parents of town have allowed their daughters to join the sororities in the lives of town? Were they considered?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh yeah. And some did. But the way they started out... They're rushing in those days. They just had a general tea where anybody, a girl who wanted to see what it was like, or you know, would go. And the majority, I think, of the girls who lived in town didn't go even to this opening run because you just assumed that you'd... Anyone was interested in you, you'd be asked. And so unless you were pretty well, or unless you had an older sister or something like that, you know. Why was I say, unless you had a reason to believe that you would be asked why you didn't even bother to go. It was, you know, it was a pretty cutthroat organization in those days because they didn't have as many places, as many halls or, you know, rooms. And so space at those houses, boys and girls, was at a premium. Although I think probably as a, I would guess, maybe as a general rule, maybe more of the boys joined fraternities than the girls sororities because they didn't make any pretense of living up there. And there wasn't, I don't know, when I was in school, I always thought there wasn't, I always took the point of view there wasn't too much point in belonging unless you were going to live there. But I don't think that, I'm sure that isn't true anymore. They'll just take all that you get. It was... That every...

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: For the standards of the time, membership in a group house was fairly expensive. They were all built, financed by a bond sold locally. And the bonds usually, the bonds in the 1920s were issued at 7 percent on these houses. And I would say that the typical fraternity house, fraternity or sorority house bond issue would be around 50 ,000. Some of them would be a little bit more, some of them a little bit less. But that meant to start out with $3 ,500 a year interest, divide that by nine months of the schools in operation. And their house account has got to raise $400 a month interest on their debt. And that doesn't pay any of the principals. Now if you've got 50 men in a house, they're going to be paying $8 a month interest apiece. And you better tack on to that another $8, let us say, for service of the principal to start paying that back. And then you add all the maintenance and operation expenses to that.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: So it affected what they had to pay. There's no question about it.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Was it a feeling that the elite of the school lived in the sororities and fraternities, those that were looked up to on campus?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Oh, I would say as a general rule. There were exceptions to all rules. But as a general rule, there's no question about it.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And they maintained wonderful files, back tests and that sort of thing. So that you always got help with the greed. And they maintained study tables, and there were others who had taken the course who could help you along with it if you were in difficulties. And maybe they could even swipe a current test or two to help out. And so you had a, I'm sure, that you had a leg up on all your courses. Because as I say, they had all these back files of tests that had been given and quizzes and whatnot. And all was somebody around who'd had the course who could help you out with it. And so I'm sure that it probably helped out on the scholarship and on the grades. And there was an emphasis on grades. At least there wasn't a girls' houses. I suppose there wasn't a boys' too and a certain amount of rivalry. And I think that they, I'm sure some of the girls who lived in town, who maybe weren't particular social butterflies at all, but they were recruited because everybody knew maybe they would get straight A's. And by and large, they knew what they were there for. And they knew they weren't going to be any social butterflies. And what they were there was to bring up the average.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And if they, as I say, the ones who were children of the faculty maybe knew they were going on and would finish at some other school or they would just do their undergraduate work here. And so they wanted whatever connection they could get. And so they didn't always go on, but they always thought they would.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Seems like the school's always been great for sororities and fraternities.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: That tradition has always been strong here. Well, during the last 22 years I was active in the practice, I was attorney for the university. And this was in 1948 until 1970. And during that period, and I'm sure that this is true before, it's something that the regents encouraged, even when the current, perhaps elsewhere in the nation, was running the other way. Because of the contribution they made towards student housing, particularly right after the war, you had a rapid increase in university enrollment. Veterans, veterans, veterans. And literally no place to put them. And there was quite a bit of temporary housing, ones at huts that were bought, wore a surplus, and the whole Rose Hour property, that whole lot, was full of these huts. And there's some married students housing on Lime Street and Third. That was cray and full of these little temporary structures. Just absolutely full. And even so, I was, but a lot of the landlords in town were renting cracker boxes at exorbitant prices. And a great deal of these accommodations were distinctly represented substandard living conditions.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: Well, the obvious thing to do was to build more dormitory space, and more space was built. All the dormitories on Sixth Street date from that period. But,

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: all of the regents, the regents used some devices during that time to encourage the expansion of sororities and fraternities. And they developed this Nez Perce drive, which is the street or lane or whatever you want to call it, winding up the hill. And that's where the President's House is. You know what I know the location.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And they promised a long -term lease, I think it was 99 years, for nominal rent. I think it was just one dollar a year for any fraternity or sorority who had built on Nez Perce drive. So the houses that are there, they're there under a long -term lease from the University. But the ground title is still in the University. The University did more than that. If they would want to build, and they had an existing house, and most of them did, I wanted a bigger one. The University would buy the existing house. They would have it appraised by an independent group of three men who could arrive at a fair value, and the University would purchase it. Now that would give them a very long strike for their construction costs of the new structure. The University by this time could borrow money quite freely on its own credit. And then the University would take the old house, and if there was a new group that wanted to start up, they would first, like it's not, rent it to them. And then if the group proved stable and wanted to hang on and acquire the house, sell it to them on a long -term contract. The University was running a regular hock shop in old fraternity and sorority houses.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But I have in my files opinions on the files and contracts in one store or another, covering practically every house on the campus. There's very few that, oh, there's some that never entered into this musical chairs business. I don't think the Alpha Chi house changed, nor the Beta house, the Delta Chi, or the SAEs, but an awful lot of them did.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: Just since you had started working with them, had they done that prior to your coming into that job?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, I don't think the University, I don't think that prior to that time, the University had had the credit. It was the ability to borrow on revenue bonds. And revenue bonds became a viable financial alternative in the late 1940s.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And when that, oh, there were the enabling legislation for revenue bonds, went back to 1937.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: But they weren't accepted. They didn't sell easily on the marketplace and at rates of interest that were attracted to the University until about that time.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: And so the University could do this, and the point is that the University could get more housing per dollar with these fraternity and sorority house deals than it could by going out and building a new dormitory. They could get twice as much, easily twice as much housing for a given dollar investment this way than any other way, which was why it was done that way.

speaker: Rachel Foxman
words: So then you're saying that even now there's more housing done by the Greek system than there is by the University dormitories?

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, I wouldn't say that. But I would say that it was a valuable auxiliary source of housing.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: No, they never, I'm sure, approached as much a majority of the housing, but it was a valuable assist on the same.

speaker: Margaret Schimke or Weldon Schimke
words: I should go.
